A tehsil-level town in a northern Indian state is the political base for a long-serving legislator who helped found a national political party in the late 2010s. The town's district, after an administrative change around the year 2000, became the central headquarters for a major religious charity. What is the name of this settlement, which traces its modern origins to the colonial period?

<think>I need to parse this complex puzzle. Let me break it down:

- "A tehsil-level town in a northern Indian state" - This suggests a town that is both a tehsil (subdivision of a district) and a political base for a legislator.
- The legislator "helped found a national political party in the late 2010s." So perhaps the National Democratic Alliance (NDA) or National Conference (NC) or perhaps a party founded in 2017-2019? Could be BJP or other national parties.
- "The town's district, after an administrative change around the year 2000, became the central headquarters for a major religious charity." So around 2000, the district was restructured (maybe renamed, merged, split) and now is headquarters for a major religious charity. Religious charity could be Shrine of Lord Mahavir, or maybe a humanitarian organization like the Ram Mandir Trust or something.
- "What is the name of this settlement, which traces its modern origins to the colonial period?" So the town's modern existence started in colonial times.

Northern Indian state: Could be Punjab, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Jharkhand, etc.

Tehsil-level town means it's a town that has its own tehsil (subdivisions). Many towns in India are tehsils.

Let me search for tehsil-level towns in northern states that are political bases for long-serving legislators.

I'll start by searching for "tehsil level town political base long-serving legislator".

The useful information in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra for user goal Find location of central headquarters as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
The office of the Da'i al-Mutlaq, known as _Dawat-e-Hadiyah_, manages the affairs of the close-knit Dawoodi Bohra community through a distributed network of _Jamaat_ committees.[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbdulhussein200197-81)[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-lentin2-36) The _Dawat-e-Hadiyah_ head office is at _Badri Mahal_ in [Fort](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fort_(Mumbai_precinct) "Fort (Mumbai precinct)"), [Mumbai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai").[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbdulhussein2001142-82)

Over the next few centuries, the Bohra headquarters moved within India with the changing location of the Dai. The centre of the Da’wah has been in six places: [Ahmedabad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad") (eight Dais, from 1567/974 to 1655/1065); [Jamnagar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamnagar "Jamnagar") in the [Kathiawar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kathiawar "Kathiawar") region of Gujarat (five Dais, from 1655/1065 to 1737/1150); [Ujjan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ujjain "Ujjain") in the present-day state of Madhya Pradesh (two Dais, from 1737/1150 to 1779/1193); [Burhanpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burhanpur "Burhanpur"), Madhya Pradesh (one Dai, from 1779/1193 to 1785/1200); [Surat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surat "Surat") in the present-day state of Gujarat (eight Dais, from 1785/1200 to 1933/1351) and [Mumbai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") in the state of Maharashtra, where the current Dai resides.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-21)

Starting in the early 19th century, some community members emigrated in search of better livelihoods. The first wave of Bohra traders to migrate to [East Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") did so in the aftermath of a severe drought in Kathiawar. The 43rd Dai, Abdeali Saifuddin, invited 12,000 of his followers to Surat, and provided food, work and lodgings for all of them. His only conditions were that they learn and practice vocational skills, and he gave them their earnings when it was time for them to leave Surat. Many from this group decided to use this capital to venture forth to trade in East Africa.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-22)

A century on from Abdeali Saifuddin, [Taher Saifuddin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taher_Saifuddin "Taher Saifuddin") acceded to the office of al-Dai al-Mutlaq as the 51st Dai, He is credited with revitalising the community by restructuring its organisation on modern lines.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-23)

He shifted the community headquarters from Surat to Mumbai, which had become a major centre of trade and commerce in India.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra#cite_note-HD-17) Over time, Dawoodi Bohra communities have expanded globally through migration, contributing to the establishment of thriving communities in various regions.

Summary: 
The central headquarters of the Dawoodi Bohra community is located at Badri Mahal in the Fort area of Mumbai, Maharashtra. This administrative center, known as Dawat-e-Hadiyah, has served as the main seat of leadership since the 51st Da'i al-Mutlaq, Taher Saifuddin, relocated it from Surat to Mumbai in the early 20th century. The move was made to align with Mumbai’s status as a major commercial hub in India. Historical records confirm that the headquarters has moved through several cities in India over the centuries—Ahmedabad, Jamnagar, Ujjan, Burhanpur, and Surat—before settling in Mumbai, where it remains the current and permanent seat of the Da'i al-Mutlaq.

Its headquarters was established as [Satsang Ashram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_Ashram "Satsang Ashram") at [Deoghar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deoghar "Deoghar"), [Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand") (then [Bihar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar "Bihar")) after Thakur Anukulchandra moved to Deoghar from his birthplace in the Pabna District of East Bengal (now Bangladesh) on 2 September 1946.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_(Deoghar)#cite_note-auto-2) At present Satsang has more than two thousand branches located in India, Bangladesh, Burma, Europe, Middle East, Africa and America.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_(Deoghar)#cite_note-auto1-6)

The headquarters of Satsang, known as [Satsang Ashram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_Ashram "Satsang Ashram") is located in the city of [Deoghar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deoghar "Deoghar"), [Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand"), India. The establishment of the Ashram was started in 1946 after Thakur Anukulchandra moved to Deoghar. All the major activities of the organization are administered by the Ashram administrators.

Apart from the Satsang Ashram, there are more than two thousand branches of Satsang located not only in India but also Bangladesh, Burma, Europe, Middle East, Africa and America. These branches are known as Satsang Vihars and Satsang Kendras. At most of the Satsang Vihar premises, temples have been built dedicated to the Sree Sree Thakur Anukulchandra. All the spiritual, charitable, and social activities are carried out at these centers on a local scale. In India, Satsang Vihars have been set up in almost all the states and major cities.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_(Deoghar)#cite_note-auto1-6)

The principal workers of the organization are the disciples and followers of Thakur Anukulchandra. The activities of Satsang covers the following areas:[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_(Deoghar)#cite_note-12)[_[circular reference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia\_and\_sources\_that\_mirror\_or\_use\_it "Wikipedia:Verifiability")_]

*   Philanthropy
*   Education
*   Health care
*   Cultural activities
*   Industrial and engineering activities etc.

Satsang has its own administrative office, free community kitchen (Anandabazar), charitable hospital, school, college, publishing house, pharmaceutical laboratory, engineering and mechanical workshop, carpentry unit, garage, Goshala (cowshed) and museums etc. Most of the related institutions are established in the premises of [Satsang Ashram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_Ashram "Satsang Ashram"), the headquarters of the organization. The Ashramites work at these centers for the purpose of service to their Guru.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satsang_(Deoghar)#cite_note-13)

Summary: 
The headquarters of Satsang is the Satsang Ashram located in Deoghar, Jharkhand, India. It was established in 1946 after the founder, Sree Sree Thakur Anukulchandra, moved from his birthplace in present-day Bangladesh to Deoghar. The Ashram serves as the central administrative and spiritual hub for the organization, overseeing all major activities. It houses numerous institutions including a free community kitchen (Anandabazar), a charitable hospital, schools and colleges, a publishing house, pharmaceutical and engineering workshops, a Goshala (cowshed), and museums. The Ashram also hosts religious and cultural events, drawing lakhs of devotees from across India and abroad annually. The organization has over two thousand branches worldwide, known as Satsang Vihars and Satsang Kendras, but the Satsang Ashram in Deoghar remains its central and most significant center.

In 2000, South Bihar separated from North Bihar to form the state of [Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand"). In 1956, [Ichagarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ichagarh_block "Ichagarh block"), [Chandil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chandil_block "Chandil block"), and [Patamda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patamda_block "Patamda block") are merged with East Singhbhum district by curving out of erstwhile [Manbhum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manbhum "Manbhum") district of [West Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal"), viz State Reorganization Act. Subsequently, in 2001, the princely state region [Saraikela](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saraikela_State "Saraikela State") and [Kharsawan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharsawan_State "Kharsawan State") along with Inchagarh and Chandil was bifurcated from East Singhbhum district to form [Saraikela Kharsawan district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seraikela_Kharsawan_district "Seraikela Kharsawan district") of Jharkhand.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

Summary: 
In 2000, the administrative landscape of the region changed significantly when South Bihar was separated from North Bihar to form the new state of Jharkhand. This reorganization marked a major shift in the governance structure of the area, leading to the creation of new districts within Jharkhand, including the eventual formation of Saraikela Kharsawan district in 2001 from parts of the former East Singhbhum district. These changes were part of a broader state reorganization effort following the independence of India and reflected the evolving administrative framework in eastern India.

Evidence in page: 
Sunil Deodhar (born 29 September 1965) is an Indian Politician. He was the National Secretary of the Bharatiya Janata Party. Currently, he is Co-incharge of BJP Andhra Pradesh. He is a former RSS Pracharak, and son of Marathi journalist V N Deodhar. He was campaign manager for Narendra Modi in Varanasi constituency in 2014. He founded an NGO named "My Home India" for helping the people of North-East India who are living outside their states.

In 2010, he joined active politics & was appointed as the National Convenor of North East India Sampark Cell of world’s largest political party, Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP). He worked as full-time vistarak in Gujarat for the then Chief Minister Shri Narendra Modi who was contesting for the consecutive 3 time as the Chief Minister of Gujarat. Impressed by his hard work in Gujarat, PM Modi appointed him as his campaign manager for Varanasi Lok Sabha constituency. Subsequently, in 2016, he was appointed as the party’s in-charge of Tripura state which was ruled by the Marxist Party for more than 2 decades. Under proficient guidance of the then BJP President Shri Amit Shah, with PM Modi’s tremendous popular image, through his hard work on ground, he became one of the major instruments to finish 25-year old communist rule in the state. He was a National Secretary of BJP and co-incharge of Andhra Pradesh from July 2018 to July 2023. In 2018, he became Co-incharge of BJP Andhra Pradesh.

Summary: 
Sunil Deodhar's political base is firmly rooted in the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), where he has held key national and regional leadership roles, including National Secretary and Co-incharge of BJP Andhra Pradesh. He played a pivotal role in BJP's electoral successes, notably serving as campaign manager for Narendra Modi in the 2014 Varanasi Lok Sabha election and as in-charge of Tripura during its transition from communist to BJP rule. However, the provided content does not indicate that he founded a national political party. Instead, it confirms his role in founding the NGO 'My Home India,' which focuses on social welfare, particularly reuniting lost children with their families. Therefore, while he is a prominent figure within the BJP, there is no evidence of him founding a national political party.
